elements.sent-2411-001: Skyrmions are nano-sized magnetic whirls attractive for spintronic applications due to their innate stability.
elements.sent-2411-002: They can emulate the characteristic behavior of various spintronic and electronic devices such as spin-torque nano-oscillators, artificial neurons and synapses, logic devices, diodes, and ratchets.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here, we show that skyrmions can emulate the physics of an RC circuit—the fundamental electric circuit composed of a resistor and a capacitor—on the nanosecond time scale.
elements.sent-2411-004: The equation of motion of a current-driven skyrmion in a quadratic energy landscape is mathematically equivalent to the differential equation characterizing an RC circuit: the applied current resembles the applied input voltage, and the skyrmion position resembles the output voltage at the capacitor.
elements.sent-2411-005: These predictions are confirmed via micromagnetic simulations.
elements.sent-2411-006: We show that such a skyrmion system reproduces the characteristic exponential voltage decay upon charging and discharging the capacitor under constant input.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, it mimics the low-pass filter behavior of RC circuits by filtering high-frequencies in periodic input signals.
elements.sent-2411-008: Since RC circuits are mathematically equivalent to the Leaky-Integrate-Fire (LIF) model widely used to describe biological neurons, our device concept can also be regarded as a perfect artificial LIF neuron.
elements.sent-2411-009: Magnetic skyrmions~\cite{nagaosa2013topological, gobel2021beyond,bogdanov1989 are a prominent focus of contemporary research.}
elements.sent-2411-010: They are whirl-like spin textures stabilized by the interplay of interactions, such as the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (DMI), the exchange interaction, and the perpendicular magnetic anisotropy (PMA).
elements.sent-2411-011: Their non-collinear magnetic moments give rise to a topological protection, quantified by the topological charge, making it possible for these textures to be observed even at room temperature~\cite{muhlbauer2009skyrmion,yu2010real,saha2024high .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Skyrmions are interesting fundamentally and for applications due to their intrinsic stability and efficient dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-013: Once stabilized in ferromagnetic materials (FM), skyrmions can be manipulated using current-induced torques such as the spin-transfer torque (STT)~\cite{zhang2004roles,sampaio2013nucleation .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The low current densities required to move them~\cite{jiang2017direct and their particle-like behavior have led to the proposal of using skyrmions as information carriers in devices like the racetrack memory~\cite{parkin2004shiftable,fert2013skyrmions , skyrmion-based applications for unconventional computing~\cite{grollier2020neuromorphic,khodzhaev2023analysis,li2021magnetic,chen2018compact,li2021magnetic , reservoir computing~\cite{everschor2024topological,msiska2023audio, pinna2020reservoir,raab2022brownian , neuromorphic computing ~\cite{song2020skyrmion,de2023biskyrmion,liang2020spiking , ratchet devices~\cite{gobel2021skyrmion,souza2021skyrmion , spin-torque oscillators~\cite{de2024circular,shen2019spin,garcia2016skyrmion and others.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: This makes skyrmions promising candidates for energy-efficient alternatives to currently used technologies.
elements.sent-2411-016: While magnetic skyrmions have been successfully employed to emulate various electronic components, such as diodes~\cite{jung2021magnetic,bindal2023antiferromagnetic,zhao2020ferromagnetic , logic gates~\cite{zhang2015magnetic,luo2018reconfigurable,liang2021antiferromagnetic , and transistors~\cite{zhang2015magnetictransistor, yang2023magnetic,zhao2018single , they have not yet been directly associated with one of the most fundamental electronic components: the RC circuit, consisting of a resistor and a capacitor.}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The latter can be charged and discharged, and it acts as a low-pass filter when alternating currents are applied.
elements.sent-2411-018: The literature about emulating this electronic circuit with magnetic textures is scarce.
elements.sent-2411-019: Ref.~\cite{chen2015rc considers domain walls, whose position mimics an RC circuit under the influence of external and demagnetizing fields.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Yet, the potential of using skyrmions in applications mimicking this traditional electronic component has remained unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we establish that skyrmions can be associated with classical RC circuits, thereby opening a new avenue for skyrmion-based applications.
elements.sent-2411-022: We show analytically that the time-evolution of the position $x$ of a skyrmion driven by currents in an engineered ferromagnetic layer is equivalent to the time-dependent voltage $V$ across the capacitor in an RC circuit, as schematically summarized in Fig.~\ref{fig:device_concept (a).}
elements.sent-2411-023: Furthermore, we demonstrate that a quadratic spatial dependence of the magnetic anisotropy parameter enables us to directly map the skyrmion's equation of motion onto the differential equation for the voltage of the capacitor.
elements.sent-2411-024: Through micromagnetic simulations, we confirm that the device concept displays the typical charging and discharging dynamics with a time response in the nanosecond range.
elements.sent-2411-025: Additionally, it exhibits the signal filtering features of RC circuits functioning as low-pass filters at GHz frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-026: To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a direct mapping between a skyrmion system and a classical RC circuit.
elements.sent-2411-027: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-028: In Sec.~\ref{sec:RC_circuits , we review the fundamental concepts of RC circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Subsequently, Sec.~\ref{sec:skyrmions_as_RC presents our device concept.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In Sec.~\ref{sec:analytical , we present and discuss the analytical results, where we show that the differential equations characterizing the skyrmion's position $x$ and the capacitor's voltage $V$ are analogous.}
elements.sent-2411-031: This step establishes the analogy $V\leftrightarrow x$ used throughout this paper.
elements.sent-2411-032: We verify these results through micromagnetic simulations in Sec.~\ref{sec:micromagnetic_simulations and demonstrate the typical features of a low-pass filter and show its application to transform waveforms depending on the frequency in Sec.~\ref{sec:micromagneticExample .}}

The racetrack is composed of a ferromagnetic (FM) layer with an engineered spatial variation of the perpendicular magnetic anisotropy (PMA), $K_{\mathrm{u}}(x)$. elements.section-2411-003: Device concept: RC-circuit analogue based on magnetic skyrmions
elements.sent-2411-061: Before we show the analytical and numerical results, we want to introduce the system unter consideration.
elements.sent-2411-062: Throughout this paper, we relate skyrmions to RC circuits by the following analogy: $V\leftrightarrow x$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Mathematically speaking, the position $x$ will take the role of the voltage $V$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Therefore, we can define the following analogies: (a) The `output' voltage $V_\mathrm{out}$ at the capacitor becomes the `output' position $x_\mathrm{out}$ of the skyrmion.
elements.sent-2411-065: The equivalence of the two seemingly far-apart systems—an RC circuit and a skyrmion racetrack—is only given if the skyrmion moves in a quadratic energy landscape.
elements.sent-2411-066: Fig.~\ref{fig:device_concept (a) depicts our device concept, featuring a nanotrack made of a ferromagnetic (FM) layer.}
elements.sent-2411-067: A Néel skyrmion is stabilized at the center, confined by the following engineered potential: It varies spatially via the perpendicular magnetic anisotropy (PMA) parameter $K_{\mathrm{u }$, which has a quadratic dependence on the position along the $x$ direction; cf.. Fig.~\ref{fig:device_concept (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-068: At the center, $K_{\mathrm{u }$ is at its minimum.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Such a variation of the anisotropy parameter could potentially be realized experimentally via focused ion beam irradiation ~\cite{juge2021helium,kern2022deterministic,ahrens2023skyrmions or a thickness modulation \cite{yu2016room, gowtham2016thickness .}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In the following Section~\ref{sec:analytical , we map the skyrmion's equation of motion onto the differential equation for the voltage of the capacitor in an RC circuit [Eq.~\eqref{eq:RC ].}}
elements.sent-2411-071: We derive the presented analogy of the voltage $V$ in the RC circuit and the position $x$ in the skyrmion system mathematically.
elements.sent-2411-072: We discuss how the energy landscape affects the skyrmion motion and how it has to be engineered to mimic the reversing force caused by the accumulation of charges on the capacitor plates.
elements.sent-2411-073: Following this mathematical framework, we validate these findings through micromagnetic simulations of the proposed device concept in Sec.~\ref{sec:micromagnetic_simulations and discuss the physical behavior of the skyrmion in detail.}

elements.section-2411-006: Example: Low-pass filter applied to square wave
elements.sent-2411-254: Before we conclude, we want to present an example of the application of the proposed skyrmion device.
elements.sent-2411-255: As we have shown, the skyrmion device can filter out high frequencies from input signals, a key feature of RC circuits under AC currents.
elements.sent-2411-256: For this reason, it has the ability of the low-pass filter to transform waveforms.

elements.sent-2411-287: These results exemplify our device concept's ability to modulate input waveforms and act as a low-pass filter.
elements.sent-2411-288: We have demonstrated a skyrmion device concept that allows us to emulate an RC circuit.
elements.sent-2411-289: We have shown analytically and via micromagnetic simulations that the skyrmion's position is analogous to the voltage at the capacitor.
elements.sent-2411-290: Under DC currents, the skyrmion position mimics the charging and discharging behavior of the capacitor.
elements.sent-2411-291: Under AC currents, it acts as a low-pass filter.
elements.sent-2411-292: In other words, the skyrmion's oscillation amplitude is attenuated at higher frequencies, allowing it to transform waveforms and filter signals.
elements.sent-2411-293: To achieve this characteristic behavior, the energy landscape in the skyrmion system must depend quadratically on the position $x$.
elements.sent-2411-294: We have engineered this potential via a quadratic dependence of the anisotropy $K_\mathrm{u (x)$ on the position $x$.}
elements.sent-2411-295: In an actual device, this parameter can be locally modulated via focused ion beam irradiation or variations in the FM layer thickness ~\cite{juge2021helium,kern2022deterministic,ahrens2023skyrmions,yu2016room, gowtham2016thickness .}
elements.sent-2411-296: Although there are more reports in the scientific literature of successfully engineering the PMA, in theory, other parameters like the DMI coefficient could also be modulated \cite{wells2017effect,balk2017simultaneous , resulting in a similar behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-297: Our results are significant from a fundamental point of view and for applications.
elements.sent-2411-298: Beyond directly resembling the electronic RC circuit, our findings further consolidate the application of skyrmions in neuromorphic computing: Artificial neurons are ideally described by the LIF (Leaky-Integrate-Fire) model that is equivalent to an RC circuit~\cite{gerstner2014neuronal .}
elements.sent-2411-299: In other words, the device concept presented in this paper also serves as a prototype for a skyrmion-based artificial neuron.
elements.sent-2411-300: The existing studies on skyrmion-based artificial neurons, such as Ref.~\cite{li2017magnetic , consider a skyrmion moving in a linear-varying PMA racetrack.}
elements.sent-2411-301: Therefore, this neuromorphic device mimics the behavior of biological neurons qualitatively but does not resemble the LIF model quantitatively.
elements.sent-2411-302: Our results motivate artificial skyrmion-based neurons that resemble the presented RC circuit dynamics so that the device concept can be used to understand and improve the potential of artificial neurons based on skyrmions.
elements.sent-2411-303: The advances in nanotrack engineering could position skyrmion systems as candidates for devices that aim to reproduce alternative fundamental electronic components.
elements.sent-2411-304: In the future, other spatial dependencies of the energy landscape can be analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-305: For example, when the PMA parameter $K_\mathrm{u}$ depends on $x$ with a higher order, an RC circuit is emulated for which the resistance depends on the voltage.
elements.sent-2411-306: In any case, replicating electronic devices based on skyrmions is an effort worthwhile because, as we have shown, the time constant $\tau_\mathrm{s =\frac{b D_0 \alpha {\pi d_z r_0^2 K_2}$ has values of a few ns and the cutoff frequency $\ws=\tau_\mathrm{s ^{-1}$ of about hundred GHz.}}}
